stem: Wei supervises a five-person research team. She has established written compliance procedures, disseminates them in writing, trains the team quarterly, and samples written communications every month for review. Despite all of this, one analyst conceals a personal trade ahead of a report by using a relative's brokerage account, which Wei uncovers only months later during a routine audit. Did Wei most likely violate Standard IV(C)?

options.A: Not a violation, because she adopted reasonable procedures and implemented an effective compliance program, and the concealed violation was not reasonably detectable given those procedures.
options.B: Violation, because the occurrence of any subordinate violation is itself proof that the supervisor's procedures were inadequate.
options.C: Violation, because supervisors are strictly liable for all subordinate misconduct regardless of the procedures they adopt.

explanation:
First Principles Thinking: reasonable procedures defense

A is correct. Handbook guidance states that a supervisor who has adopted reasonable procedures and taken steps to institute an effective compliance program may not be in violation of Standard IV(C) even if a violation occurs despite those efforts. Wei's documented procedures, training, and sampled communications reviews describe exactly that posture; the analyst's use of a relative's account to conceal the trade defeats ordinary detection.

B treats IV(C) as strict liability in practice. The standard is explicit that occurrence of a violation may indicate procedures are inadequate, but does not automatically make them so.

C is the purest overreach: it abandons the 'reasonable efforts' test the standard sets and converts it into unlimited vicarious responsibility.

stem: Mendez, senior vice president and head of research at a regional broker-dealer, decides to change her recommendation on Timber Products from buy to sell. Following firm policy, she orally advises other executives of the pending change before the report is published. She has not, however, established procedures restricting those who learn of the change from trading or disclosing it. One direct report immediately sells Timber shares from a discretionary client account, and others tip select institutional clients before publication. Which statement is most accurate?

options.A: Not a violation, because she complied with her firm's pre-notification policy by orally advising executives.
options.B: Violation, because she failed to establish procedures to prevent the dissemination of, or trading on, a pending recommendation change by those informed.
options.C: Not a violation, because the trading and tipping decisions were made independently by subordinates acting on their own.

explanation:
First Principles Thinking: the supervisor must build information barriers

B is correct. Standard IV(C) requires supervisors to establish procedures for preventing and detecting violations most likely to occur in their area of responsibility. A pending change in recommendation is precisely the moment when front-running and selective disclosure are most likely; the absence of information-barrier procedures means Mendez did not take reasonable supervisory steps, even though she followed the firm's pre-notification policy.

A hides behind policy compliance. Following one policy does not discharge a supervisor from creating others that her role obviously requires.

C attempts to push responsibility downstream to the subordinates. The standard squarely places the design-and-prevent duty on the supervisor, whose failure created the environment in which the misconduct could occur.

stem: Larsen heads a 120-person operations group. Because personally overseeing every employee is impracticable, he delegates day-to-day supervision to six team leads, instructs each in writing on the firm's compliance procedures and on specific methods for detecting and preventing violations, and reviews each team lead's monitoring logs monthly. Did Larsen most likely violate Standard IV(C)?

options.A: Violation, because Standard IV(C) obligations cannot be discharged through any form of delegation to subordinate supervisors.
options.B: Violation, because he does not personally review the activities of every individual employee in the group.
options.C: Not a violation, because delegation to subordinate supervisors is permitted when accompanied by clear compliance instructions and ongoing review of the delegates' work.

explanation:
First Principles Thinking: delegation with instruction and review

C is correct. Handbook guidance explicitly recognizes that members with oversight of large numbers of employees may delegate supervisory duties to subordinates, provided they instruct those subordinates on methods to promote compliance and oversee the discharge of those duties. Larsen's written instructions and monthly review of monitoring logs track this guidance directly.

A flatly forbids delegation. The standard's own guidance does the opposite: it contemplates delegation as a necessary mechanism for effective supervision at scale.

B requires personal evaluation of every employee, which Handbook guidance rejects as impracticable in large organizations and therefore not required.

stem: Rios is president of a registered investment advisory firm that trades S&P 500 Index futures to hedge market risk across several funds and its employee profit-sharing plan. He assigns a senior portfolio manager to handle all futures trading but has not established written operating procedures or a compliance manual governing futures trading, and the firm's compliance department does not review such trades. Over time the manager uses delayed account designations to allocate favorable fills to one favored account. Which statement is most accurate?

options.A: Violation, because Rios failed to establish adequate recordkeeping and reporting procedures sufficient to prevent or detect the manager's allocation misconduct.
options.B: Not a violation, because once a portfolio manager has been assigned to a trading function, the firm president's personal supervisory responsibility ends.
options.C: Not a violation, because the misallocation was accomplished through deceptive timing that no supervisor could reasonably have foreseen.

explanation:
First Principles Thinking: supervision at the firm-procedures level

A is correct. Standard IV(C) requires supervisors to establish, document, and enforce compliance procedures covering employee trading activity, especially in areas like futures allocations where conflicts are foreseeable. A firm with no written procedures and no compliance review of futures trading fails the basic structural requirements of the standard, and the president who assigned the activity bears that supervisory failure.

B misstates the scope of delegation. Assigning a trading task to a portfolio manager does not transfer the duty to create the underlying compliance framework.

C claims unforeseeability where the risk is in fact standard: delayed account designation and favorable-fill allocation is a well-known abuse pattern the supervisor must anticipate.

stem: Abara manages a small advisory team and has supervisory responsibility for day-to-day trading. Firm-wide compliance policies are set by senior management, and Abara has no authority to change them. She identifies that the firm's trade-allocation policy is materially inadequate and promptly submits a written memorandum to senior managers describing the deficiency and recommending specific corrective action. Senior management has not yet acted. Did Abara most likely violate Standard IV(C)?

options.A: Violation, because she retains supervisory responsibility and therefore bears liability for any deficiency regardless of the steps she has taken.
options.B: Not a violation, because Standard IV(C) requires a supervisor lacking authority to modify firm-wide policies to bring the deficiency to senior managers' attention and recommend corrective action, which is what she has done.
options.C: Violation, because upon identifying the deficiency she was required to resign her supervisory role immediately.

explanation:
First Principles Thinking: supervisor without firm-wide authority

B is correct. Handbook guidance addresses this situation explicitly: a supervisor who lacks authority to establish or modify firm-wide compliance policies should work through the firm structure by bringing the inadequacy to senior managers and recommending corrective action. Abara has done exactly that.

A imposes strict liability regardless of the supervisor's actual authority, which the standard rejects.

C overstates the escalation requirement. Resignation (or declining the role) is reserved for cases in which the supervisor cannot meaningfully discharge her duties at all, not for ordinary deficiencies she has escalated and is pursuing corrective action on.

stem: Hoffman is compliance officer at a wealth-management firm responsible for advisers across multiple branches. He has not adopted any policies or procedures addressing how advisers should assess suitability for complex structured products. An adviser at one branch, Delgado, routinely recommends a complex feeder-fund product to retirees with low risk tolerance, many of whom suffer significant losses when the feeder fund's underlying strategy unwinds. Which statement is most accurate?

options.A: Not a violation, because individual recommendations and their suitability are the responsibility of the recommending adviser rather than compliance.
options.B: Not a violation, because Hoffman cannot reasonably be expected to anticipate every product an adviser may recommend.
options.C: Violation, because Hoffman's inadequate policies, procedures, training, and supervision allowed unsuitable recommendations of a complex product, which is a supervisory failure under Standard IV(C).

explanation:
First Principles Thinking: supervisor responsibility for preventing foreseeable misconduct

C is correct. Handbook guidance places the burden on compliance officers with supervisory scope to adopt policies and procedures for recurring risk areas, including suitability assessments for complex products. Hoffman's failure to do so is the structural defect that enabled Delgado to recommend unsuitable products to conservative clients, making it his supervisory failure under Standard IV(C).

A misallocates the duty. The adviser's own recommendation duty coexists with the supervisor's obligation to put guardrails in place; the two are not mutually exclusive.

B invokes unforeseeability, but suitability issues around complex products are among the most foreseeable supervisory risks and are the archetype of what written procedures must address.

stem: Okonkwo is offered a supervisory role over a newly launched derivatives desk. Before accepting, he reviews the firm's compliance infrastructure for that desk and concludes that it is so inadequate that he could not meaningfully discharge supervisory duties if he took the position. He declines the role and explains his reasons in a memo to senior management. Which statement is most accurate?

options.A: Not a violation, because when a member cannot discharge supervisory responsibility due to an absent or inadequate compliance system, Standard IV(C) permits the member to decline the role until reasonable procedures are in place.
options.B: Violation, because declining an offered supervisory role is itself a breach of the duty of loyalty to the employer.
options.C: Violation, because a supervisor is expected to accept the role and then fix the compliance system from within.

explanation:
First Principles Thinking: the option to decline supervisory responsibility

A is correct. Handbook guidance explicitly provides that if a member clearly cannot discharge supervisory responsibilities because of the absence of a compliance system or because of an inadequate compliance system, the member should decline to accept supervisory responsibility until the firm adopts reasonable procedures. Okonkwo's decision mirrors the standard exactly.

B reframes the issue under Standard IV(A), but even there the duty of loyalty does not require accepting an unworkable supervisory role.

C reverses the Handbook's guidance, which prefers declining the role over accepting it with inadequate tools.

stem: Chung is head of trading and Patil is chief compliance officer. A junior trader, Amari, comes to each of them separately with specific evidence suggesting that the firm's flagship hedge fund has not reflected recent market losses in its reported performance. Both Chung and Patil tell Amari not to ask questions, saying the fund is 'too big and too successful' to be her concern. Which statement is most accurate?

options.A: Not a violation, because supervisors are entitled to rely on the firm's established reporting processes and need not treat every junior trader's concern as credible.
options.B: Not a violation, because Amari has no direct supervisory authority over the hedge fund's operations.
options.C: Violation, because Standard IV(C) requires supervisors and compliance officers to review and investigate concerns brought to their attention, and summary dismissal of such concerns undermines the firm's ethical operations.

explanation:
First Principles Thinking: duty to investigate concerns raised from below

C is correct. Handbook guidance treats a supervisor's dismissal of a credible concern about potential misconduct as a direct violation of Standard IV(C). The supervisor and compliance officer together have the responsibility to review and investigate the concerns brought forth, and to foster an ethical culture in which such concerns are taken seriously rather than suppressed.

A hides behind 'established processes,' but the standard asks what the supervisors themselves did in response to a specific credible warning, not whether they generally trust firm systems.

B shifts the focus to Amari's authority, which is irrelevant. The duty to investigate runs from the supervisor and compliance officer downward, not from the reporting employee upward.

stem: Alvarez, a supervisor, learns that a subordinate may have executed a mismarked trade. She immediately convenes an internal assessment, retrieves the relevant trade records, places the subordinate on restricted trading pending the outcome of the review, and reports the matter to the firm's compliance department. Did Alvarez most likely violate Standard IV(C)?

options.A: Violation, because restricting the subordinate's trading is a premature sanction before any finding of misconduct.
options.B: Not a violation, because she promptly initiated an assessment, placed reasonable interim limits on the alleged offender pending the outcome, and escalated the matter to compliance.
options.C: Violation, because she should have deferred all action until the subordinate either confirmed or denied the allegation.

explanation:
First Principles Thinking: supervisor's response to a suspected violation

B is correct. Handbook guidance requires a supervisor who learns of a potential violation to promptly initiate an assessment of the wrongdoing, place limits on the alleged offender's activities pending the outcome, and escalate appropriately. Alvarez's response tracks these requirements precisely.

A reframes a mandated interim measure as an improper sanction. The standard specifically calls for restricting the alleged offender pending investigation, so restriction is not a premature penalty but a protective step.

C argues for deference to the subordinate's own statements, but the Handbook warns that relying on the employee's assurances is not enough and that active investigation is required.

stem: Kovac learns that a subordinate has likely breached firm trading rules. He reports the matter to the compliance department and verbally warns the subordinate not to repeat the conduct. He then resumes normal supervision, placing no restrictions on the subordinate's activities and making no effort to determine how widespread or long-running the behavior may have been. Which statement is most accurate?

options.A: Violation, because merely reporting the misconduct and warning the employee, without investigating the extent of the wrongdoing or placing interim limits on the subordinate, is insufficient under Standard IV(C).
options.B: Not a violation, because reporting the conduct to the compliance department discharges the supervisor's obligations under Standard IV(C).
options.C: Not a violation, because warning the employee is the normal first step in addressing minor rule breaches.

explanation:
First Principles Thinking: reporting and warning are not enough

A is correct. Handbook guidance is emphatic that reporting the misconduct to compliance and warning the employee to cease the activity are not sufficient. The supervisor must also promptly investigate the extent of the wrongdoing and place interim limits on, or increase monitoring of, the offender pending the outcome. Kovac has done none of these things.

B treats the compliance referral as a full discharge, which the standard explicitly rejects.

C trivializes the issue as a minor first offense, but the Handbook's 'insufficient' language applies regardless of how minor the supervisor believes the conduct to be.

stem: Schmidt oversees a 400-person fixed-income division at a large bank. He cannot personally evaluate each employee's conduct on a continuing basis, so he delegates supervision through a documented hierarchy of desk heads and team leads, ensures each layer is trained on compliance methods, commissions periodic independent reviews of monitoring quality, and reviews escalated issues himself. Did Schmidt most likely violate Standard IV(C)?

options.A: Violation, because the standard requires a supervisor to personally verify the conduct of every employee within the organization.
options.B: Violation, because delegating supervision across multiple layers inherently dilutes accountability and therefore violates Standard IV(C) per se.
options.C: Not a violation, because for large organizations Standard IV(C) contemplates delegation through a documented hierarchy supported by instruction, training, and periodic review.

explanation:
First Principles Thinking: scaled supervision in large organizations

C is correct. Handbook guidance states that members with oversight responsibility for large numbers of employees may not be able to personally evaluate each one and may delegate supervisory duties, provided they instruct subordinates on compliance methods and oversee how those duties are carried out. Schmidt's documented hierarchy, training, independent reviews, and personal review of escalations all fit this framework.

A insists on personal evaluation of every employee, which the standard expressly does not require for large organizations.

B condemns multilayer delegation as inherently improper, which is the opposite of the Handbook's position.

stem: Reyes, director of research, hires a junior analyst and encourages her to 'expand the firm's social media presence' to share investment ideas with clients. Reyes has not established any firm policies governing the use of personal or firm social media accounts, or the dissemination of research through them. The new analyst, excited about a company she is drafting a buy report on, posts a preview of her recommendation on her personal account before the report is reviewed. Which statement is most accurate?

options.A: Not a violation, because a junior analyst's personal social media activity falls outside the supervisor's scope.
options.B: Violation, because Reyes failed to establish policies and procedures to prevent the unauthorized dissemination of research through social media before assigning the analyst online responsibilities.
options.C: Not a violation, because the dissemination problem was created by the analyst's own impulsive posting rather than by any supervisory act.

explanation:
First Principles Thinking: procedures must precede the activity

B is correct. Standard IV(C) requires compliance procedures to be in place before the conduct they are meant to govern occurs. Encouraging social media activity without first establishing policies on research dissemination through online channels is precisely the structural failure the Handbook identifies as a supervisory violation.

A pretends that personal accounts are outside the supervisor's reach, but the Handbook treats business-related online activity as squarely within the supervisor's policy-setting duty.

C blames the analyst for the resulting post, but the supervisory failure is the absence of rules and training that would have prevented it in the first place.

stem: Ng is head of equity research at a large firm. A trader in a wholly separate trading division, over whom Ng has no supervisory, managerial, or reporting authority, executes a series of illegal front-running trades. Ng becomes aware of the conduct only after the fact, through a market rumor. Is Ng most likely in violation of Standard IV(C)?

options.A: Not a violation, because Standard IV(C) applies only to persons subject to the member's supervision or authority, and the offending trader is subject to neither.
options.B: Violation, because any CFA charterholder who becomes aware of misconduct anywhere in the firm must treat it as a supervisory matter.
options.C: Violation, because awareness of the conduct converts her into a de facto supervisor of the trader under Standard IV(C).

explanation:
First Principles Thinking: scope of supervisory responsibility

A is correct. Standard IV(C) binds members who have employees subject to their supervision or authority. A trader in a separate division outside Ng's reporting chain is not under her control or influence, so the supervisory duty under IV(C) is not triggered. (She may have other reporting duties under different standards, but not IV(C) itself.)

B expands IV(C) to every CFA member who learns of misconduct, which the standard's 'subject to their supervision' language does not support.

C invents a de facto supervisory relationship based on awareness alone. The Handbook grounds supervisory duties in actual authority or control, not in knowledge.

stem: Ortiz, a vice president responsible for compliance in the trading department, has a compensation package partly tied to commission revenues generated by that department. She notices a sharp and unusual increase in trading volume in a stock that is not on the firm's recommended list, a pattern consistent with irregular activity. She takes no steps to investigate or halt the activity. Which statement is most accurate?

options.A: Not a violation, because incentive-based compensation is a standard industry practice and does not impair supervisory judgment.
options.B: Not a violation, because she is not personally executing any of the trades in question.
options.C: Violation, because she failed to investigate a clear sign of potential misconduct and must be especially sensitive to conflicts between her self-interest and her supervisory responsibilities.

explanation:
First Principles Thinking: conflict-sensitive supervisory duty to investigate

C is correct. Handbook guidance specifically flags that supervisors whose compensation creates a conflict with their supervisory duties must be especially sensitive to that conflict and must not allow it to deter investigation of potential misconduct. Ortiz's inaction in the face of a visible irregular-activity pattern is a Standard IV(C) violation.

A dismisses the conflict as unremarkable. The Handbook treats the conflict itself as a reason for heightened, not relaxed, supervisory vigilance.

B reframes the issue around personal participation in the trades, which is irrelevant; the violation is her failure to investigate indicators of misconduct she had a duty to follow up on.

stem: Bakshi, a compliance-focused supervisor, has implemented regular ethics and compliance training, issues quarterly compliance reminders, incorporates professional-conduct evaluations into each employee's annual performance review, and periodically updates the firm's written procedures in response to new risks. Did Bakshi most likely violate Standard IV(C)?

options.A: Violation, because written procedures must be updated continuously rather than only periodically.
options.B: Not a violation, because she is carrying out the post-implementation supervisory activities the Handbook expressly contemplates, including dissemination, periodic updates, education, reminders, and performance-review integration.
options.C: Violation, because incorporating professional conduct into performance reviews improperly blurs ethical and business evaluations.

explanation:
First Principles Thinking: model post-implementation supervisory practices

B is correct. Handbook guidance lists exactly the steps Bakshi is taking as appropriate once a compliance program is in place: dissemination, periodic updates, continual education, periodic reminders, incorporation of professional-conduct evaluation into performance reviews, and ongoing monitoring. Bakshi's program is a template for compliance with Standard IV(C).

A invents a continuous-update requirement. The Handbook asks for periodic updates, not constant ones.

C claims that mixing ethics with business metrics is improper, but the Handbook explicitly encourages incorporating professional-conduct evaluation into performance reviews as a way to build a culture of integrity.

stem: Prakash is CEO of a broker-dealer with overall supervisory responsibility. One of his direct reports routinely provides price quotes on illiquid bonds to a significant institutional customer. Prakash is aware that this quote-provision activity is occurring but has not developed or trained employees on any policies or procedures governing how such quotes are prepared, documented, or reviewed. The trader begins supplying artificially inflated quotes that the customer uses to misstate the valuation of its portfolio. Which statement is most accurate?

options.A: Violation, because Prakash failed to develop, promote, and train employees on adequate policies governing the provision of price quotes, and therefore failed to supervise the trader's activities to prevent and detect the misconduct.
options.B: Not a violation, because Prakash did not personally prepare or approve any of the inflated quotes.
options.C: Not a violation, because the customer, not Prakash's firm, was the party actually misstating the valuations.

explanation:
First Principles Thinking: knowledge of activity triggers policy-building duty

A is correct. Handbook guidance places squarely on the supervising CEO the duty to develop, promote, and train employees on adequate policies governing an activity he knows is occurring. Prakash's awareness of the quote-provision business coupled with the absence of any policy framework is the classic supervisory failure Standard IV(C) addresses.

B retreats to a hands-off standard, but the supervisory duty attaches to the creation of rules and training, not personal approval of every action.

C blames the downstream customer, which is irrelevant to whether the upstream supervisor built the necessary guardrails around his own firm's conduct.

stem: After reviewing a recent industry enforcement matter involving a manipulation technique not previously seen at her firm, Harper drafts and rolls out new written procedures specifically targeted at detecting and preventing that technique, trains affected staff on them, and integrates them into the firm's monitoring program. No violation of that type has yet occurred at her firm. Which statement is most accurate?

options.A: Violation, because Standard IV(C) requires that compliance procedures be adjusted only after a violation has already occurred.
options.B: Violation, because adopting targeted procedures before a violation occurs signals that the supervisor believed misconduct was already imminent.
options.C: Not a violation, because effective compliance procedures should be in place prior to the occurrence of a violation and should anticipate the activities most likely to result in misconduct.

explanation:
First Principles Thinking: anticipatory design of compliance procedures

C is correct. Handbook guidance states that compliance procedures must be in place prior to any legal or ethical violation and that, while they cannot anticipate everything, they should be designed to anticipate the activities most likely to result in misconduct. Harper is doing precisely this.

A inverts the standard. The Handbook explicitly calls for proactive procedures rather than post-incident adjustments.

B characterizes proactive supervision as an admission of wrongdoing. The standard treats anticipation as prudence, not as implication.

stem: Moreau, a supervisor, has championed two complementary documents at her firm: a short, principle-based code of ethics written in plain language and addressed to all employees, and a separate, detailed compliance manual containing the specific policies and procedures through which the code is implemented. Which statement is most accurate?

options.A: Violation, because Standard IV(C) requires that codes of ethics and compliance procedures be consolidated into a single document.
options.B: Not a violation, because a plain-language, principle-based code of ethics kept separate from detailed compliance procedures is the structure the Handbook describes as most effective.
options.C: Violation, because detailed compliance manuals are exclusively the responsibility of regulators rather than supervisors.

explanation:
First Principles Thinking: code of ethics separate from compliance procedures

B is correct. Handbook guidance explicitly draws a distinction between a principle-based code of ethics and the detailed policies and procedures required to implement it. It warns that mingling compliance procedures into the code of ethics defeats the code's purpose of conveying ethical ideals in plain language. Moreau's two-document structure is exactly what the Handbook recommends.

A reverses the Handbook's preference for a separated structure.

C shifts compliance-manual responsibility away from the firm to regulators, which the Handbook does not contemplate; supervisors are responsible for drafting and maintaining adequate compliance procedures for their own firms.

stem: Ikeda, head of the trading desk, is approached by a junior analyst who has noticed unusual pricing patterns in the firm's flagship fund. Rather than dismissing the concern, Ikeda takes detailed notes, escalates the matter in writing to the compliance department, requests a review of the relevant trades, and places temporary restrictions on related activity pending the outcome. Did Ikeda most likely violate Standard IV(C)?

options.A: Not a violation, because she took the subordinate's concern seriously, promptly escalated it for investigation, and placed appropriate interim limits on related activity pending the outcome.
options.B: Violation, because placing interim limits on activity based on an unproven allegation is itself an overreach.
options.C: Violation, because a supervisor must first complete the investigation before imposing any interim restriction.

explanation:
First Principles Thinking: model response to a whistleblower concern

A is correct. Standard IV(C) requires supervisors to review concerns raised by employees, promptly initiate assessment of potential misconduct, and take interim protective steps pending the outcome. Ikeda's written escalation, request for review, and temporary activity limits line up with each of these requirements.

B reframes a prescribed protective step as an overreach. The Handbook specifically calls for interim limits when misconduct is suspected.

C postpones action until after investigation, which is the opposite of what the Handbook requires.

stem: Alvim supervises a team of data engineers, none of whom are CFA charterholders or candidates. He ensures that they are trained on, and comply with, the firm's ethical and compliance policies, including rules on material nonpublic information they may encounter in their work. Which statement is most accurate?

options.A: Violation, because Standard IV(C) requires supervisors to apply its requirements only to employees who are themselves CFA charterholders or candidates.
options.B: Violation, because extending compliance training to non-investment staff exceeds the scope of Standard IV(C).
options.C: Not a violation, because Standard IV(C) applies to any investment professional who exercises control or influence over employees regardless of whether those employees are CFA members or candidates.

explanation:
First Principles Thinking: supervisory duty extends beyond CFA members

C is correct. Handbook guidance is explicit: any investment professional who has employees subject to her or his control or influence, whether or not those employees are CFA Institute members, charterholders, or candidates, exercises supervisory responsibility under Standard IV(C). Alvim is therefore required to do exactly what he is doing.

A limits the standard's reach to CFA members, which the Handbook specifically rejects.

B characterizes appropriate supervision of non-investment staff as overreach. In fact, it is what the standard requires.

stem: Costa has rolled out a performance-review process in which every employee in her group is evaluated not only on business metrics but also on a professional-conduct component weighted meaningfully alongside financial goals. She uses the framework to reinforce a culture of integrity and to ensure that compliance behavior influences promotion decisions. Which statement is most accurate?

options.A: Violation, because mixing ethical and financial metrics in a single review process dilutes the objectivity of both assessments.
options.B: Not a violation, because incorporating a professional-conduct evaluation into an employee's performance review is expressly contemplated by the Handbook as an element of reasonable supervision.
options.C: Violation, because only the compliance department may evaluate professional conduct under Standard IV(C).

explanation:
First Principles Thinking: embedding ethics in incentive structure

B is correct. Handbook guidance urges supervisors to incorporate a professional-conduct evaluation as part of an employee's performance review, because tying compensation and promotion decisions to ethical behavior is among the most effective ways to build a culture of integrity. Costa's design is directly aligned with this guidance.

A treats the integration as a dilution, reversing the Handbook's position that the integration itself is the point.

C strips line supervisors of any role in professional-conduct evaluation, but the standard explicitly assigns that role to supervisors as part of reasonable oversight.
